title: SAML vs OIDC: how an SIS does single sign-on
summary: Universities don't want another password. Your SIS must federate to a campus identity provider via SAML or OpenID Connect. Today: what each protocol is, the roles involved, and the classic SP-initiated SSO flow you'll integrate against.

activities.title: Two protocols, one goal
activities.markdown:
**SSO** lets a user authenticate once at an **Identity Provider (IdP)** and access many apps. Two standards dominate:

- **SAML 2.0** — an **XML-based** standard for exchanging authentication & authorization data between an **IdP** and a **Service Provider (SP)** using signed **assertions**. It became an **OASIS** standard in **March 2005**.
- **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** — a modern authentication protocol built **on top of OAuth 2.0** (RFC 6749/6750), published by the **OpenID Foundation**. The app is the **Relying Party (RP)**; the IdP is the **OpenID Provider (OP)**.

Rule of thumb: legacy campus IdPs often speak **SAML**; newer apps and mobile prefer **OIDC**.

sources.title: How OpenID Connect Works — OpenID Foundation

activities.question: OpenID Connect is built on top of which framework?
activities.explanation: OIDC is an authentication layer on top of the OAuth 2.0 authorization framework (RFC 6749/6750), adding identity via an ID token.

sources.title: How OpenID Connect Works — OpenID Foundation

activities.question: In a SAML SSO flow, which party authenticates the user and issues the assertion?
activities.explanation: SAML defines three roles: the principal (user), the IdP (authenticates and issues assertions), and the SP (consumes the assertion to grant access).

activities.question: What does an OpenID Provider return to assert the user's identity?
activities.explanation: OIDC returns an ID token (a signed JWT) — usually alongside an access token — and exposes user claims at the UserInfo endpoint. SAML's equivalent is the XML assertion.

sources.title: How OpenID Connect Works — OpenID Foundation

activities.title: SAML 2.0 SP-initiated SSO
activities.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[User requests SP resource] --> B[SP redirects to IdP SSO service]
  B --> C[IdP authenticates the user]
  C --> D[IdP issues a signed SAML assertion]
  D --> E[Browser POSTs assertion to SP]
  E --> F[SP validates and grants access]

steps.title: Request the resource
steps.caption: The user hits a protected page at the Service Provider (your SIS).

steps.title: Redirect to IdP
steps.caption: The SP redirects the browser to the campus IdP with an AuthnRequest.

steps.title: IdP authenticates
steps.caption: The user signs in at the IdP (the SP never sees the password).

steps.title: Signed assertion
steps.caption: The IdP issues a digitally signed SAML assertion about the user.

steps.title: POST to SP
steps.caption: The browser POSTs the assertion back to the SP's assertion consumer service.

steps.title: Access granted
steps.caption: The SP validates the signature and establishes a session.

activities.caption: OIDC's Authorization Code flow is the analogous modern dance: redirect → authenticate → code → ID token.

activities.front: SAML **assertion** vs OIDC **ID token** — both prove identity, but how do they differ?
activities.back: A **SAML assertion** is a signed **XML** document carrying authentication/attribute/authorization statements. An **OIDC ID token** is a signed **JWT** (compact JSON). Same purpose, different serialization and ecosystem.

items: **OIDC**: identity on top of **OAuth 2.0** (OpenID Foundation), using **ID tokens (JWTs)**.
**SP-initiated SSO**: request → redirect to IdP → authenticate → signed token → SP grants access.
An enterprise SIS usually must support **both** to onboard universities with mixed IdPs.

glossary.definition: The party that authenticates the user and issues assertions/tokens (e.g., a campus SSO).
Service Provider (SAML) / Relying Party (OIDC) — the application that relies on the IdP to authenticate users.
A signed XML packet of security statements (authentication, attribute, authorization decision) about a subject.
A signed JWT issued by an OpenID Provider asserting the end-user's identity in OIDC.

reflection: Universities arrive with mixed IdPs. Would you front ManageArk with SAML, OIDC, or both — and how does federated identity map onto OpenFGA so a user's campus identity resolves to tenant-scoped permissions?
